name: Nut
description: Nut, also known as Nuit, is the Egyptian goddess of the sky. She is depicted as a woman arched over the earth, her body covered in stars. Nut is the daughter of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture) and the wife of Geb (earth). Her children include Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder. According to myth, Ra, the sun god, decreed that Nut could not give birth on any day of the year. Thoth, the god of wisdom, gambled with the moon and won five extra days, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children on these newly created days. Nut's role is crucial in Egyptian cosmology; she swallows the sun god Ra each evening, allowing him to travel through her body during the night, and then gives birth to him each morning, marking the cycle of day and night. She protects the earth from the chaos of the primordial waters. She represents the heavens, the cosmic mother, and is associated with rebirth and protection in the afterlife. Nut is often depicted on the inside of sarcophagus lids, symbolizing her protective embrace of the deceased in their journey to the afterlife. Her star-studded body represents the vastness of the cosmos and the eternal cycle of life, death, and rebirth. She is a powerful and revered figure in Egyptian mythology, embodying the sky and its mysteries.
subtitle: Goddess of the Sky and Heavens

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Nut's Children
keyMyths.description: Ra forbade Nut from giving birth on any day of the year, so Thoth won five extra days from the moon, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children.
keyMyths.source: The Egyptian Book of the Dead

keyMyths.title: Nut Swallowing the Sun
keyMyths.description: Each evening, Nut swallows the sun god Ra, who travels through her body during the night and is reborn each morning.

keyMyths.title: Separation of Nut and Geb
keyMyths.description: Originally locked in an embrace, Shu, god of air, separated Nut (sky) and Geb (earth) to create space for life.

sources.author: R.T. Rundle Clark

sources.description: Explores the symbolism and meaning behind Egyptian myths.
